steps.title: Energy you store by lifting
steps.body:
Lift something up and you've stored energy in it — gravity is ready to give it back the moment you let go.

A raised hammer, water held behind a dam, a roller coaster at the top of the hill: all carry **gravitational potential energy**. The higher and heavier, the more.

steps.title: The formula
steps.body: **m** is mass (kg), **g** is gravity's pull (**9.8 m/s²** on Earth), and **h** is height above your chosen zero line.

steps.title: Get a feel for it
steps.body: Lift the block and slide its mass around. Watch how stored energy climbs steadily with both — then try to hit the goal.

steps.prompt: You lift the same box to twice the height. What happens to its gravitational potential energy?
hints: U_g = mgh — height appears once (not squared).

steps.prompt: Plug in the numbers. A **2 kg** book sits **1.5 m** up (g = 9.8). Drag mass and height into their spots, then find U_g.
steps.fillHint: Mass goes in the m spot, height in the h spot. Here m = 2 and h = 1.5.

steps.title: Zero is your choice
steps.body: Here's the twist: **h is measured from a zero line you pick**. Move the zero line and U_g changes. Only **changes** in U_g (between two points) are fixed.

steps.prompt: Same book, same spot — but you redefine the floor as **1.5 m higher**, so the book is now at h = 0. Drag **U_g**.
hints: U_g depends on your zero line.
At the reference level, h = 0 → U_g = 0 J.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You sized gravitational PE and saw why the reference level matters.
